description:
You are a junior backup–operator who has just received a small sandbox on
    /home/user.  
    A colleague has already placed a test folder on the system so you can
    practise doing a very basic “backup-and-restore” cycle.

    The directory tree CURRENTLY looks like this:

      /home/user/backup_demo/
      └── original/
          └── sample.txt

    1. The file /home/user/backup_demo/original/sample.txt already exists and is
       readable by you.  Do NOT modify its contents.

    2. Your assignment is to prove that you can:
       • create a compressed backup archive,
       • simulate a disaster (by deleting the live copy),
       • restore the archive to a different location,
       • and record the integrity hash of the restored file.

    3. VERY IMPORTANT:  When you finish, the system must comply with the
       following exact state so that the automated grader can verify your work.

       a) A compressed archive called
          /home/user/backup_demo/backup/backup.tar.gz must exist.  
          – It must contain exactly one directory “original” with exactly one
            file “sample.txt” inside it.
          – The archive must be a standard GNU tar file compressed with gzip.

       b) A restoration directory called
          /home/user/backup_demo/restore/ must exist and inside it the file
          sample.txt must have been extracted so that its content is identical
          to the original.

       c) The original directory at
          /home/user/backup_demo/original/ must no longer exist (pretend it was
          lost in the “disaster”).

       d) A plain-text log file
          /home/user/backup_demo/restore_test.log must exist and contain EXACTLY
          the two lines below (no extra spaces, tabs, or blank lines):

          FILE: sample.txt
          SHA256: <64 lowercase hexadecimal characters>

          Substitute “<64 lowercase hexadecimal characters>” with the real
          SHA-256 hash of the restored sample.txt file.

          Example of correct formatting (obviously with a different hash):
          FILE: sample.txt
          SHA256: 0123456789abcdef…(total 64 chars)…abcdef

    4. You are expected to accomplish the task with roughly three to four
       standard shell commands (tar, rm, mkdir, sha256sum, etc.).  You do NOT
       need root privileges for anything.

    5. No screen output or text besides what is required in
       /home/user/backup_demo/restore_test.log will be graded, but feel free to
       print progress messages to the console for your own debugging.

    When everything matches the specification above, the exercise is complete.
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the agent starts:

    1. Directory: /home/user/backup_demo/
    2. Sub-directory: /home/user/backup_demo/original/
    3. File: /home/user/backup_demo/original/sample.txt
       Content (5 bytes, **no trailing newline**):
         hello

    Exact byte sequence of sample.txt:
        68 65 6c 6c 6f

    SHA-256 hash of that exact content:
        2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824

    Verification logic used by grader:

    • Confirm that /home/user/backup_demo/backup/backup.tar.gz exists and, when
      extracted, contains original/sample.txt with the SHA-256 hash above.

    • Confirm that /home/user/backup_demo/original/ does NOT exist after the
      agent’s commands have run.

    • Confirm that /home/user/backup_demo/restore/sample.txt exists and its
      SHA-256 hash equals
      2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824.

    • Confirm that /home/user/backup_demo/restore_test.log exists and contains
      exactly (including newline at end of second line):

      FILE: sample.txt\n
      SHA256: 2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824\n

      where “\n” stands for the Unix newline (0x0A).

    All directories under /home/user/backup_demo/ are owned by the current
    non-root user and are writable.
name: task_000000_40deac2d
